                                                               CALENDAR OF FEATURES
                                          In addition to the monthly data appearing regularly in Employment and
                                          Earnings, special features appear in most of the issues, as shown below:

                                                                        Jan.   Feb.   Mar.   Apr.   May   July   Aug.   Sept.    Oct.   Nov.
                     Household data
                  Annual averages                                        X
                  Revised seasonally adjusted series and
                   current seasonal factors                                     X
                  Quarterly averages:
                   Seasonally adjusted data
                   Persons not in labor force                            X                    X            X                      X
                   Vietnam Era war veterans
                     Establishment data
                  National annual averages:
                   Industry divisions (preliminary)                      X
                   Industry detail (final)                                             X
                  Women employment (National)                                   X                    X            X                      X
                  National data adjusted to new benchmarks                                                                       (1)
                  Revised seasonally adjusted series and
                   current seasonal factors                                                                                      (1)
                  State and area annual averages                                                     X
                  Area definitions                                                                   X

                     1
                       The issue that introduces the establishment data adjusted to new benchmarks varies. The October 1972 issue
                 marks the introduction of March 1971 benchmarks.

                                                                                                           *By Thomas F. Bradshaw



               A new body of data on the methods unemployed             method used most often, the type and number of job-
           persons used to find work has recently become available      seeking methods used did vary according to the char-
           and will be published as a regular series in Employment      acteristics of the jobseekers. For example: prime-age
           and Earnings beginning with the present issue. This new      (20-54 years) jobseekers tended to use a wider variety of
           series should aid significantly the analysis of current      methods than either teenagers or elderly workers; blacks
           labor force behavior and may be especially useful to         went to public employment agencies more often than
           manpower planners and personnel directors. This intro-       whites, and whites were more likely to use private agen-
           ductory note discusses the basis for this series and sum-    cies; women relied more heavily on newspaper ads than
           marizes a few of the findings over the 1970-71 period. 1     men. An examination of the data for seasonal patterns
               Data on methods unemployed persons used to find          showed that although no distinct pattern existed, there
           work are obtained from a question in the Current Popu-       did appear to be a tendency towards the use of public
           lation Survey concerning what the respondent who             employment agencies in the winter months and friends
           looked for work during the reference period did to find      and relatives in the summer. As additional data are
           work. This question was first asked in the survey in Janu-   obtained, analysis of these, and other trends, will be

           indicate they are looking for work actually perform a

                     Data relate to employees in such occupations in the telephone industry as switchboard operators; service assistants; operating room instructors; and pay-station attendants. In 1971, such

                  t As noted on page 7 of the January 1972 issue of this magazine, the series does not include earned pay withheld as a consequence of delays caused by the wage freeze in effect between Au-
           gust 15 and November 14, 1971, and by administrative procedures of the Pay Board. These delays resulted from compliance with the Government's Economic Stabilization Program. Because these
           withheld payments were substantial and fell due under a significant number of new labor-management contracts in that industry, they affected a large proportion of SIC 372. Accordingly, the series
           will be supplemented by publication in the future of a series which will include withheld payments in the months earned.

                       Subarea of Washington, D.C. Standard Metropolitan Statistical Area: Alexandria, Fairfax, and Falls Church cities and Arlington, Fairfax, Loudoun, and Prince William Counties, Virginia.
                       Not available.
                  **   Revised to 1972 benchmark; not comparable with data published in table B-7.

                   NOTE: Data are adjusted to 1972 benchmark levels with these exceptions: Colorado and Denver (1971); Massachusetts and areas (1970); Michigan and areas (1971); North Carolina and areas
                   (1971); and Fargo-Moorhead, North Dakota (1971).

                                                                     Introduction

               The statistics in this periodical are compiled from three major     whereas detailed industrial classifications can be reliably derived
           sources: (1) Household interviews, (2) reports from employers,          only from establishment reports.
           and (3) administrative statistics of unemployment insurance                 Data from these two sources differ from each other because
           systems.                                                                of differences in definition and coverage, sources of information,
               Data based on household interviews are obtained from a              methods of collection, and estimating procedures. Sampling
           sample survey of the population 16 years of age and over. The           variability and response errors are additional reasons for
           survey is conducted each month by the Bureau of the Census for          discrepancies. The major factors which have a differential effect
           the Bureau of Labor Statistics and provides comprehensive data          on levels and trends of the two series are as follows:
           on the labor force, the employed and the unemployed, including
           such characteristics as age, sex, color, marital status, occupa-
           tions, hours of work, and duration of unemployment. The
                                                                                   Employment
           survey also provides data on the characteristics and past work
           experience of those not in the labor force. The information is          Coverage. The household survey definition of employment
           collected by trained interviewers from a sample of about 47,000         comprises wage and salary workers (including domestics and
           households, representing 461 areas in 923 counties and                  other private household workers), self-employed persons, and
           independent cities, with coverage in 50 States and the District of      unpaid workers who worked 15 hours or more during the survey
           Columbia. The data collected are based on the activity or status        week in family-operated enterprises. Employment in both
